listings.dealer: Corsinos Service Station and Repair Inc

top10.introHtml: Scouring Cars.com, CarGurus, and AutoTrader within 35 miles of Media, PA for used convertibles priced $7,000–$15,000. Here are the 10 best buys for a fun second car — ranked by value, condition, driving character, and sanity of ownership.

picks.whyGood: The Z3 2.3 is the sweet spot of the model run — enough power to be genuinely fun, M-derived engine architecture, and a chassis that rewards a spirited back-road drive. At $9,990 with only 75k miles and a clean CarFax, this is what the whole budget convertible exercise is for. Selden Motors is a known local independent and the Great Deal pricing badge confirms it's below market.
picks.whatToVerify: Inspect the soft top for tears and rear window delamination — replacement is $600–$1,200. Check for rusty subframe mounting points (common on Z3s older than 2000). Verify cooling system service history; the 2.3 six can run hot if the thermostat and expansion tank are original.
picks.bottomLine: The definitive pick. Pure sports-car DNA at a fair price, clean title, no accidents, and close by.

picks.whyGood: A 4.2L V8 quattro all-wheel-drive cabriolet for $7,400 is a genuinely absurd value proposition. The B7 S4 Cabriolet was a $70,000 car new; this one is verified clean title, no accidents, priced $3,000 below comparable listings. The 340hp 4.2 FSI is a howling, naturally aspirated masterpiece.
picks.whatToVerify: Budget for maintenance: the timing chain tensioners on the 4.2 FSI are a known issue and service runs $2,000–$3,500 if not done. Ask for records. Convertible top hydraulic pump seals can fail ($400–$800). Factor in premium fuel and 18 mpg combined.
picks.bottomLine: Extraordinary car for the money — but go in eyes open on German V8 running costs. If the timing chain has been done, this is a steal.

picks.whyGood: The R230 SL 500 was Mercedes' flagship two-seat roadster — a $93,000 car in 2004. This one has just 53,000 miles, clean title, no accidents, and is priced under $10k. The V8 5.0L and retractable hardtop (ABC suspension included) are the SL's signature features. Genuinely rare to find one with this mileage at this price.
picks.whatToVerify: The ABC (Active Body Control) hydraulic suspension is expensive to fix when it fails — $1,500–$4,000 depending on severity. Check for the telltale oil puddles under the front suspension. Get the roof cycling confirmed: it should open and close cleanly in under 16 seconds. Ask for service history.
picks.bottomLine: Sub-$10k for a 53k-mile V8 SL roadster with clean history is hard to argue with. Pre-purchase inspection is mandatory — budget $200 for that peace of mind.

picks.whyGood: 21,150 miles on a 2005 Z4. Read that again. This car has barely been driven — that's 1,000 miles a year average. The E85 Z4 2.5i is one of the great pure driver's roadsters of the 2000s, with near-50/50 weight distribution and a silky inline-six. At 21k miles it's essentially a time-capsule car. Private seller listing means room to negotiate.
picks.whatToVerify: With such low mileage, ask specifically about storage conditions and whether rubber components (hoses, belts, tires) have been replaced even if they look unused — age affects rubber regardless of mileage. Verify no long-term storage damage. Get a full inspection.
picks.bottomLine: The mileage alone makes this exceptional. If the car checks out on inspection, it's an easy buy at $13,000.

picks.whyGood: 45,000 miles is remarkably low for a 2004 — this car has barely been used. The 1.8T is a bulletproof turbocharged four with a massive aftermarket, tunable, and well-understood. The B6 Cabriolet has one of the most elegant convertible body designs of its era. Clean title, no accidents confirmed.
picks.whatToVerify: Check the timing belt — the 1.8T is an interference engine and it's due every 75k miles or 5 years (whichever first). At 22 years old, even at low mileage the belt should have been replaced by age. Convertible top hydraulic pump and mechanism should be tested through several full cycles.
picks.bottomLine: Exceptional low-mileage buy. Verify timing belt service and you have a sorted, elegant convertible for under $10k.

picks.whyGood: The R171 SLK has aged beautifully — tight, sporty roadster feel with a folding hardtop that completely eliminates the fabric-top compromise. The SLK 280's 3.0L V6 (228hp) is a sweet, rev-happy engine. Great Deal pricing, clean title, no accidents, only 16 miles away.
picks.whatToVerify: Test the retractable hardtop mechanism thoroughly — both opening and closing. SLK roof actuators can develop issues ($400–$1,200). Check for any transmission slip in the 7G-Tronic automatic. Verify that the panoramic roof glass (if equipped) has no cracks.
picks.bottomLine: Underrated and often overlooked in favor of flashier options. The SLK 280 hardtop convertible at this price is a confident daily driver that also happens to be a blast on a sunny Saturday.

picks.whyGood: The MR2 Spyder is the wild card on this list — a 2,200-lb mid-engine roadster that's essentially a street-legal go-kart. Toyota reliability, parts availability, and a legendary handling balance make this a unicorn find. At 56,500 miles with no accidents, it's barely half-used. Only 16 miles away.
picks.whatToVerify: The ZZW30 MR2 has a reputation for snap oversteer at the limit — fun for experienced drivers, catches beginners off guard. Inspect the soft top carefully: MR2 Spyder tops are hard to find and expensive. Check for any clutch wear (it has a short throw and light pedal).
picks.bottomLine: If you want pure driving experience and don't need a back seat or trunk, there is nothing on this list more fun per dollar than the MR2 Spyder.

picks.whyGood: The C70 is the practical convertible: a retractable hardtop, four genuine seats, and Volvo safety engineering wrapped in an understated Swedish design that doesn't scream 'mid-life crisis.' The T5's 2.5L turbocharged five-cylinder (227hp) is torquey and pulls hard. 74k miles and no accidents from a Ford dealer.
picks.whatToVerify: The power retractable hardtop mechanism has multiple motors and sensors — cycle it several times and ensure it latches correctly. Check for any P-PZEV emissions control codes. T5 timing belt is due every 7 years/105k miles; verify it's been done on a car this age.
picks.bottomLine: The anti-showoff convertible. If you want to drop the top and enjoy the drive without attracting attention, the C70 is supremely comfortable and unassuming.

picks.whyGood: 11,656 miles on a 2003 Z4 — this is essentially a brand-new car from 22 years ago. At $12,385 from Fred Beans (a reputable PA dealer group), this is one of the lowest-mileage Z4s you will ever find. The 2.5i with the manual transmission is the purist's choice.
picks.whatToVerify: Same caution as the low-mile 2005 Z4: verify that age-sensitive components (coolant hoses, belts, tires, rubber mounts) have been proactively replaced. A car with 11k miles is still a 22-year-old car. Full PPI from an independent BMW specialist.
picks.bottomLine: Two low-mileage Z4s in the top 10 is remarkable — the 2003 at $12,385 with 11k miles makes it another collector-grade find. One to move on quickly.

picks.whyGood: Ford's retro Thunderbird revival (2002–2005) was a limited-production collectible from day one — only 68,000 were built across all four model years. The 2002 with 54k miles and no accidents is a stylish weekend cruiser that stands out from every other car in this list. The 3.9L V8 is smooth and the porthole hardtop is period-correct.
picks.whatToVerify: Thunderbird values have been gradually appreciating — verify current market comparables before negotiating. Check that the removable porthole hardtop is included. The convertible top should cycle cleanly; power roof actuator issues are known. The 5R55S automatic transmission has a service history requirement.
picks.bottomLine: The cool pick. Fewer than 68,000 exist; they're getting harder to find at this price. A fun, distinctive weekend car that's starting to attract collector interest.

mustangs.introHtml: Five Mustang convertibles turned up within range — from a pristine 44,000-mile 1994 GT to a modern 2018 S550. Something for every era of pony car fan.

picks.whyGood: 44,000 original miles on a 1994 GT is the kind of find that doesn't come along often. The SN95 5.0 GT makes 215hp with a classic pushrod V8 soundtrack, clean title, no accidents, and private seller — room to negotiate. At $10,500 for a 44k-mile GT, this is priced below the market for a well-preserved example.
picks.whatToVerify: At 32 years old: inspect floor pans and trunk for rust (common SN95 rust points). Verify the 5.0 hasn't been modified in ways that compromise reliability. Check convertible top and rear window carefully — SN95 tops are getting harder to source.
picks.bottomLine: The best Mustang on the list. 44,000 miles is exceptional for a car this age — worth the drive to see it.

picks.dealer: Corsinos Service Station and Repair Inc
picks.whyGood: The Fox-body Mustang GT is pure American muscle icon. The 302 (5.0) HO V8 with a 4-barrel carb makes a real sound. At $9,500 with Great Deal pricing and no accidents, this is a clean Fox-body at an honest price. Only 16 miles away.
picks.whatToVerify: Fox bodies are legendary for rust — check the floors, frame rails, and convertible top well. Inspect the soft top for tears (Fox tops deteriorate). The 302 is bulletproof if maintained; verify coolant and oil service. At $9,500 you're buying a 38-year-old car — PPI is worth it.
picks.bottomLine: Classic Fox-body character at a real-world price. Good for a weekend driver who appreciates the era.

picks.whyGood: The 2014 gets the last of the classic SN197 styling before the S550 facelift. The V6 Premium convertible is well-equipped with the 305hp Cyclone V6 — more than adequate, better on gas than the GT, and less target for insurance. 79k miles is reasonable for the price.
picks.whatToVerify: Accident history not confirmed from this source — pull a full vehicle history report before buying. Inspect the convertible top seals and latches; the 2014 top can develop wind noise issues at highway speeds.
picks.bottomLine: The modern daily-driver Mustang option — still stylish, more practical, and the V6 is genuinely good.

picks.whyGood: Nine miles away, $11,459, private seller. The 2012 V6 is the same 305hp Cyclone engine in a slightly older body. West Chester is a convenient test-drive. Higher mileage but priced accordingly.
picks.whatToVerify: Pull history report — accident status not confirmed from CarGurus data. Check the convertible top operation carefully at 96k miles.
picks.bottomLine: The local budget pick. Close enough to go see on a whim, priced for the mileage.

picks.whyGood: The S550 generation (2015+) brought independent rear suspension and modern tech to the Mustang. At $12,992 this is the most modern Mustang on the list. The mileage is high at 141k but the 2018 S550 is known to go 200k+ with basic maintenance.
picks.whatToVerify: High mileage means closer attention to maintenance records: verify timing chain service, transmission fluid changes, and rear IRS bushings. Pull a vehicle history report — accident status unknown. At 141k miles, budget $500–$1,000 for immediate maintenance.
picks.bottomLine: Best modern tech for the money, but the miles are real. Worth it if the history report is clean and maintenance is documented.
